title: Żurek
subtitle: Polish sour rye soup
excerpt: A thick, tangy Polish soup based on fermented rye flour, the sourness is the whole flavour. Ladled over sliced smoked sausage, hard-boiled egg, and boiled potato, it is the Easter soup of Poland.
sourceNotes: Żurek (also called żur) is one of the oldest Polish soups, based on a fermented rye flour baste (zakwas) that gives it its distinctive sourness. Documented in Maria Lemnis and Henryk Vitry, Old Polish Traditions in the Kitchen and at the Table (1979) and Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989). The soup is the traditional first course of Easter Sunday breakfast in Poland.

content.text: Żurek is built on a fermented rye flour liquid called zakwas. The fermentation takes 2 to 3 days and produces a tangy, slightly funky base that is unlike anything else in European cooking. The soup itself, built on a pork or chicken stock, thick with the zakwas, and served over smoked sausage, hard-boiled egg, and potato, is one of the most satisfying and distinctive things in the Polish kitchen.

If you find the fermentation step daunting, żurek concentrate is sold in Polish delicatessens and online (look for 'zakwas na żurek' or 'żurek w butelce'). A 500 ml bottle makes this soup straightforwardly, use it in place of the homemade zakwas in the same quantity.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: boiled in salted water until tender, sliced

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: taste before adding, the sausage and stock are salty

items.name: Dried oregano
items.prepNote: dried marjoram is traditional if you can find it

content.text: Make the zakwas (2 to 3 days ahead)

In a clean jar, whisk the rye flour into the warm water until smooth. Add the crushed garlic, allspice, and bay leaves. Stir well. Cover loosely with cloth or a lid ajar, the fermentation needs air. Leave at room temperature for 2 to 3 days, stirring once or twice daily. The liquid will turn cloudy, sour-smelling, and slightly bubbly. It is ready when it smells tangy and sour. Strain before using.

Bring the chicken stock to a simmer in a large saucepan. Add the crushed garlic, smoked sausage, and oregano. Simmer for 10 minutes. Add the strained zakwas (or bottled concentrate) in a steady stream, stirring as you pour, the soup will thicken visibly. Simmer gently for 10 minutes. Do not let it boil hard. Stir in the horseradish sauce. Taste and season with salt.
Divide the sliced potato and sausage between four deep bowls. Ladle the hot żurek over the top. Place two hard-boiled egg halves in each bowl. Serve with rye bread.

items.fix: Place in a warmer spot (top of the fridge, or near a radiator); the fermentation needs 18 to 22°C
items.cause: Temperature too cold, or the flour was old

items.fix: Dilute with more stock; the sourness should be assertive but not sharp to the point of discomfort
items.cause: Zakwas over-fermented, or too much was added

items.fix: Simmer uncovered for 10 more minutes to reduce slightly; the rye flour in the zakwas is the thickening agent

content.text: Żurek is the Easter soup, eaten at the end of the Easter vigil or for breakfast on Easter Sunday, after the Lenten fast. It often arrives in a hollowed bread bowl (chleb żurkowy) in restaurants. The sour rye base is one of the oldest fermented foods in Central and Eastern European cooking, predating both bread-making and dairy fermentation in the region.
